coredata.dc:title: Construction of cathode-free iron-ion batteries by one-step synthesized carbon nanodots using FeO(OH) deposition
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Shuai
Li, Zhenqian
Wang, Meng
Jia, Dongyi
Jin, Haiyi
Zhang, Han
Zhang, Haipeng
Ma, Yixuan
coredata.dc:description: The development of high-performance and cost-effective battery technologies is crucial for storing and utilizing intermittent renewable energy. Aqueous iron-ion batteries (AIBs) constructed using low-cost iron salts hold promise in energy storage strategies but are constrained by the complex design and construction of cathode materials. Here, we report a one-step synthesis of carbon nanodots as conductive substrates for cathode-free aqueous iron-ion batteries, enabling the efficient deposition of hydroxyl iron oxide and enhanced ion storage. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), and Raman analyses confirm the effective deposition of hydroxyl iron oxide, ensuring the achievement of a maximum capacity of 0.43 mAh cm−2. The designed cathode-free aqueous iron-ion battery demonstrates a long cycling life of up to 2000 cycles, representing a new direction for the development of aqueous iron-ion batteries.

The authors thank the staff from SSRF at BL02U2.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A, SEM observations and b, elemental distribution images and c, EDS spectra for CND.
A, 2D diffraction patterns and b, XRD patterns obtained by integration. c, Raman spectra.
A, TEM images on large numbers of CND clusters and b, localized individual CND/secondary particles. c, HR TEM observations on CND.
A, Initial capacity of the assembled battery and b, capacity after 10 cycles, shown in the form of charge/discharge curves.
A, SEM observations and b, elemental distribution images and c, overlapping plotted elemental distribution maps. d, EDS spectra, which show the elemental content.
2D XRD patterns and XRD patterns obtained by integration.
A, Raman spectra evidence for the formation of FeO(OH). b, Raman spectra of CND at high wave numbers.
Long cycle performance of assembled cathode-free cell. The inset shows the structure of FeO(OH).

A, FeO(OH) deposition on carbon cloth electrode without CND addition. The capacity comparison between carbon cloth electrode with CND addition and carbon cloth electrode without CND addition is presented in the form of charge/discharge curves.

head.ce:title: Construction of cathode-free iron-ion batteries by one-step synthesized carbon nanodots using FeO(OH) deposition

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical and Electrical Engineering, Hetao College, Bayannur 015000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Mechanical and Electrical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Mechanical and Electrical Engineering, Hetao College, Bayannur 015000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Digital Industry, Jiangxi Hongzhou Vocational College, 331100 Fengcheng, China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Xi’an University of Technology, Xi’an 710048, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of high-performance and cost-effective battery technologies is crucial for storing and utilizing intermittent renewable energy. Aqueous iron-ion batteries (AIBs) constructed using low-cost iron salts hold promise in energy storage strategies but are constrained by the complex design and construction of cathode materials. Here, we report a one-step synthesis of carbon nanodots as conductive substrates for cathode-free aqueous iron-ion batteries, enabling the efficient deposition of hydroxyl iron oxide and enhanced ion storage. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), and Raman analyses confirm the effective deposition of hydroxyl iron oxide, ensuring the achievement of a maximum capacity of 0.43 mAh cm−2. The designed cathode-free aqueous iron-ion battery demonstrates a long cycling life of up to 2000 cycles, representing a new direction for the development of aqueous iron-ion batteries.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Rechargeable batteries provide reversible conversion between electrical and chemical energy, which is critical for powering mobile electronic devices and storing intermittent energy [1–3]. The pursuit of green energy, particularly renewable energy, has further heightened demands for battery technologies with lower costs and enhanced safety [4–6]. Aqueous batteries, which utilize aqueous solutions of metal salts as electrolytes and the corresponding metals as anodes, have emerged as promising candidates [7–9]. Their inherent non-flammability and the abundance of resources have garnered increasing attention [7]. Among various aqueous battery technologies, iron-ion batteries stand out due to the inherent advantages of iron: its low cost and abundance [10,11]. Compared to other common metals, iron is an order of magnitude cheaper [10]. The maturity of the iron smelting industry further amplifies this cost-performance advantage. However, compared to zinc ion batteries [12–14] and other aqueous batteries [15] that have been extensively studied, the development of iron ion batteries is still quite preliminary. Consequently, developing high-performance aqueous iron-ion batteries is highly desirable and warrants further and broader efforts.

The development of high-capacity, long-lifespan cathodes is key to advancing iron-ion battery technology. Feasible battery applications have already been demonstrated with vanadium-based oxides [16], phosphates [17], and iodine [18]. For example, efforts to explore this family of materials have recently expanded to Prussian Blue Analogue (PBA) materials, as reported in FeHCF and other insoluble Prussian blue (IPB) [19]. Notably, Mai et al. revealed a unique hydroxyl iron oxide deposition mechanism in iron-ion batteries [20]. Their study demonstrated that the hydroxyl iron oxide deposited during cycling can provide additional capacity by storing protons, effectively doubling the capacity. Furthermore, they confirmed that this deposition phenomenon is independent of the structural design or composition of PBAs and extended this finding to polyoxoanionic compounds such as Na4Fe3(PO4)2(P2O7) (NFPP), which have distinctly different structures.

This unique phenomenon inspired us to consider a novel possibility: utilizing only the hydroxyl iron oxide deposited during cycling as the positive electrode to achieve a so-called “cathode-free” configuration in aqueous iron-ion batteries. Clearly, preparing the positive electrode during battery cycling rather than through pre-synthesis could significantly reduce manufacturing costs and process complexity. However, such an attempt has not yet been made.

Here, carbon nanodots (CNDs) were synthesized in bulk from benzene using a one-step combustion method, coated onto carbon cloth as a conductive substrate to construct a cathode-free aqueous iron-ion battery. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) with energy-dispersive spectroscopy (EDS), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and Raman spectroscopy analyses confirmed the nanoscale size and composition of the carbon dots, while X-ray diffraction (XRD) characterization revealed their amorphous nature. After several cycles, Raman spectroscopy, XRD, and SEM analyses verified the effective deposition of hydroxyl iron oxide, which facilitated a continuous increase in capacity and the achievement of a maximum capacity of 620mAh g−1 (calculated based on CNDs) and 0.43 mAh cm−2 (calculated based on cathode area). Benefiting from the continuous deposition and the stable ion storage capacity of hydroxyl iron oxide, a cycling lifespan of up to 2000 cycles was achieved in the assembled cathode-free aqueous iron-ion battery, representing an excellent record for iron-ion batteries. Hydroxyl iron oxide deposition on pure carbon cloth was also demonstrated to corroborate the reliability of the proposed mechanism. Utilizing a low-cost deposition process to achieve stable, high-performance electrodes provides an intriguing demonstration for advancing aqueous iron-ion batteries.

Materials: FeSO4, benzene purchased from Aladdin. Iron foil was purchased from Alfa Aesar. Carbon cloth was purchased from Toray. All the chemicals were obtained and used directly without further purification. To prepare CND, benzene was burnt in air and a stainless-steel mesh was placed over the burnt benzene to collect the CND obtained by incomplete combustion. After the stainless-steel mesh was covered with a black substance, it was placed in distilled water to cool down and wash off the black substance and the collection was continued. Finally, the distilled water mixed with the black substance was filtered and washed with acetone to obtain CND. the obtained CND was left in an oven overnight to dry. Finally, the CND was carefully scraped off the filter membrane and stowed away.

Characterization: Electrodes were characterized with TEM (JEM-F1000), SEM (JEOL JSM-7100F), and Raman spectrometer (RENISHAW inVia Basis 532 nm). XRD patterns were obtained at the SSRF-BL02U2 beamline.

Electrode preparation: To prepare the electrodes, CND and PVDF in a mass ratio of 9:1 was mixed and ground in a mortar and pestle until a homogeneous mixture was formed, which was then carefully scraped onto the carbon cloth. The scraping was done in two passes to fully saturate the mixture and the carbon cloth. Subsequently, the carbon cloth was placed in a vacuum oven at 60 degrees Celsius overnight.

Electrochemical measurements: CND coated on carbon cloth was assembled as a cathode-free cell for further measurements. The cells were assembled in a 2032 coin with CND as the cathode substrate, Whatman GF/B as the diaphragm, iron foil as the anode, and 1 M FeSO4 as the electrolyte. The batteries were cycled between 0.1–1.0 V at Land battery test system (LAND CT2001A). The current density was calculated as the mass of CND.

The carbon nanodots (CNDs) were synthesized via one-step combustion of benzene in air. SEM imaging revealed that CNDs were fine and homogeneous in morphology with uniform stacking throughout the field of view, which facilitated the filling of voids in the carbon cloth collector to form a reliable conductive substrate (Fig. 1 a). EDS analysis of the prepared CNDs showed a predominant presence of carbon along with trace amounts of oxygen (Fig. 1b). Trace amounts of oxygen may come from the adsorption of air oxygen by the CNDs, which is supported by the EDS spectra showing an intensity much lower than the carbon peak of the oxygen peak also corroborates this (Fig. 1c). The distribution of carbon closely matched the contours observed in SEM images, confirming the successful synthesis of the material and its uniform elemental distribution.

The structure of the CNDs was analyzed using X-ray diffraction (XRD) and Raman spectroscopy. The 2D diffraction patterns collected at the Shanghai Synchrotron Radiation Facility 02U2 showed no distinct diffraction rings, and the integrated curves lacked sharp Bragg peaks (Fig. 2 a). A broad feature was observed at approximately 10 degrees, typically attributed to the (002) peak of low-crystallinity carbon materials (Fig. 2b) [21]. This indicates that the synthesized CNDs are predominantly amorphous. Raman spectroscopy further examined the vibrational modes of the bonds in the material. Peaks at 1345 cm−1 and 1590 cm−1 Raman shifts were identified, corresponding to D and G, respectively (Fig. 2c) [22]. The calculated ID/IG ratio was 0.9 (calculated by intensity), suggesting a low degree of crystallinity, consistent with the XRD results.

The detailed structure of the CNDs was examined using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). TEM images revealed CND clusters similar to those observed in SEM, with individual CND particles clearly visible and showing distinguishable edges (Fig. 3 a). The size of the CND is uniform and consistent, about 33–54 nm, and clusters together to form a grape-like secondary structure (Fig. 3b). High resolution (HR) TEM analysis did not display any discernible lattice structures, consistent with the amorphous nature indicated by the XRD results (Fig. 3c).

The CNDs were mixed with polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) binder and thoroughly stirred in N-Methylpyrrolidone (NMP) to form a slurry, which was then coated onto carbon cloth to prepare the CND@C electrode. Thanks to the high carbon content, no additional conductive agent was required in the slurry. The electrode was paired with an iron anode and FeSO4 electrolyte to assemble an aqueous battery, followed by initial charge–discharge tests. As shown, almost no capacity (<40mAh/g, calculated based on the CND mass) was observed during the first discharge, indicating that the CNDs cannot store iron ions, only a small amount of adsorption (Fig. 4 a). However, after several cycles, the capacity gradually increased to 90mAh/g, with expanded profile appearing in the charge–discharge curves (Fig. 4b). According to a previous report by Mai et al., hydroxyl iron oxide can grow on the cathode during high-potential charging due to electrolyte decomposition (Fe2++3OH–-e− → FeO(OH) + H2O), providing sites for proton storage [20]. The theoretical capacity of single electron transfer is expected to be 301mAh/g. Thus, the capacity increase is attributed to the formation of hydroxyl iron oxide, which will be analyzed in detail in the following sections. Notably, since the prepared CND@C electrode does not inherently provide capacity, the battery's operation relies on the hydroxyl iron oxide formed during cycling, thereby eliminating the need for complex initial cathode preparation and offering cost advantages.

Powder was scraped from the electrodes after several cycles and observed using SEM. In addition to the well-characterized CND, some unusual blocky particles were found embedded within the CND (Fig. 5 a). Elemental mapping via EDS revealed strong iron peaks in the spectrum (Fig. 5b). The elemental distribution indicated that these blocky particles mainly consisted of iron and oxygen, clearly distinguishable from the surrounding CND (Fig. 5c). Considering that the CND contains no iron, these iron oxides were confirmed to originate from the electrolyte. Notably, no sulfur signals were detected, ruling out crystallization of ferrous sulfate (Fig. 5d). Further analysis of these particles requires additional testing methods.

XRD analysis of the powder revealed that, in addition to the amorphous carbon signals, several bright diffraction rings appeared in the 2D XRD patterns (Fig. 6 ). The integrated curve showed that these sharp crystalline signals, such as (1 1 0), (1 1 1), and (2 2 1), matched well with FeO(OH) (#81–0463) [23]. This confirms that hydroxyl iron oxide (FeO(OH)) was indeed formed on the prepared conductive substrate during cycling, contributing to the observed capacity. The isotropic diffraction rings further indicated that the crystalline FeO(OH) exhibits a polycrystalline nature [23].

Raman spectroscopy was also used to investigate the vibrational modes in FeO(OH). The results revealed distinct vibrational peaks at 240 cm−1, 297 cm−1, 394 cm−1, 475 cm−1 and 543 cm−1, differing from the peak patterns of CND. These peaks were attributed to B1g (240 cm−1, 543 cm−1) and Ag vibrations (297 cm−1, 394 cm−1, 475 cm−1), confirming the formation of FeO(OH) (Fig. 7 a) [24]. The clear identification of these vibrational modes further corroborates the reliable formation of FeO(OH) during cycling. In addition, the D and G mode peaks belonging to CND can still be observed at high wave numbers, indicating the preservation of the CND structure (Fig. 7b). Together, these analyses provide solid evidence of FeO(OH) generation, which serves as the foundation for achieving long cycling stability and high performance.

Benefitting from the high-performance cathode obtained from within the battery, the assembled cathode-free cell can remarkably run for over 2,000 cycles, which is the first cathode-free aqueous iron ion battery as far as we know (Fig. 8 ). More importantly, this achievement was made without the need for complex material designs, as previously done, but instead by simply assembling the necessary components and allowing them to naturally form through electrochemical cycling. Cycling tests show that the maximum capacity (based on CND mass) reaches 620 mAh/g, with an areal capacity of 0.43 mAh cm−2.

In comparison, we also prepared a carbon cloth electrode without CND addition. After a long cycling, although FeO(OH) deposition between fibers was also observed (Fig. 9 a), the highest capacity could only be reached at ∼ 0.14mAh cm−2 (Fig. 9b). This comparison highlights the positive effect of CND loading on the facilitation of the deposition reaction and capacity retention.

In this work, we report the development of a novel long-life cathode-free aqueous iron-ion battery by one-step synthesis of carbon nanodots (CND) as the conductive substrate. SEM, XRD, and Raman spectroscopy collectively confirm the effective deposition of hydroxide iron oxide, which is crucial for achieving high-performance batteries. This resulted in an ultra-long cycle life of 2000 cycles as the first cathode-free aqueous iron ion battery prepared. The cathode-free configuration of the aqueous iron-ion battery provides an interesting and attractive approach for further developing advanced battery technologies, offering reliable insights for researchers in the field.

Shuai Zhang: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation. Zhenqian Li: Formal analysis. Meng Wang: Data curation, Conceptualization. Dongyi Jia: Validation, Resources, Conceptualization. Haiyi Jin: Investigation. Han Zhang: Supervision. Haipeng Zhang: Writing – review & editing. Yixuan Ma: Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by Talent Introduction Scientific Research Foundation of Hetao College (No. HYRC202315), Science and Technology Planning Project of Bayannur (No. KL2024063) and Science and Technology Research Project of Hetao College (No. HYYB202308). The authors thank the staff from SSRF at BL02U2.
